message.content:
See ~/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/single-sequencer-consolidated/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw

How would you improve the architecture of this codebase, especially if the goal was to squeeze even more performance out of it, noting that the most important items coming up are:

- adding durability to the logs and the corresponding "replay"
- improving performance likely via zero copy behaviour across the board (inc to network and disk stacks)

correctness, performance, latency - no compromise

Serious job, don't do it all yourself, use both codex and claude agents to make suggestions here

We have golden fixtures and benchmarks to keep any attempts honest

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- ai-config: Use when working in the ai-config repo, editing portable agent skills, changing Claude/Codex/Cursor config, or maintaining ai-setup, ai-doctor, ai-sync, ai-adopt, and ai-push.
- bottling: Pin, name, and re-enter agent contexts with the `bottle` CLI. Use when the user says "bottle this as X", "rebottle this", "decant X into a pane", "pin this context", "pin this session", "save this conversation as a bottle", or wants to snapshot the current session and resume it later from exactly this point.
- design-an-interface: Produce 3+ radically different designs for a module, API, or interface, compare them in prose, and synthesize a recommendation. Use when asked to "design an interface", "shape this API", "design it twice", "explore module boundaries", or when planning a new deep module and the first idea is unlikely to be the best. Based on "Design It Twice" from A Philosophy of Software Design — the value is the contrast, not the first draft.
- domain-modeling: Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.
- grilling: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design. Use when the user wants to stress-test a plan before building, or uses any 'grill' trigger phrases.
- herder: Mechanics for driving herdr surfaces — create workspaces / worktrees / tabs / panes, spawn named GUID-tagged agents (claude, codex, bash, etc.) into panes or tabs with verified prompt delivery, message and wait on running peers, track them via a local registry, and cull them safely. Use when the user says "spawn a <role> agent", "cull that agent", "list spawned agents", "open a worktree pane", or any other request to provision or manage herdr surfaces. Plumbing only — multi-session run protocols (topologies, playbooks, verification) live in the `orchestrate` skill, which builds on this one.
- herder-fork: Fork the current claude or codex session into a new herdr pane via the herder. Use when the user says "fork this", "fork right", "fork below", "fork the session", "branch this session", or wants to spawn a forked copy of the active agent next to the current pane. Companion to the `herder` skill.
- improve-architecture: Use when reviewing a package/directory for architectural friction, deepening a shallow module cluster, or improving testability. Triggers "let's review packages/X", "how should we refactor Y", "improve architecture of Z", or complaints that a module is hard to understand or has brittle/over-mocked tests. Produces a napkin issue file; does NOT write implementation code.
- k6-load-testing: Run k6 load/performance tests against the local v4-offchain stack and measure backend throughput/latency limits. Use when asked to load test, stress test, benchmark, find the breaking point, or measure latency-vs-load of the trading backend locally. Encodes the auth/signer, rate-limit, margin, and order-book traps that otherwise produce wrong numbers.
- orchestrate: Run a long or complex plan across multiple agent sessions — compose a per-run protocol from a menu of topologies (sequential phases, relay, fan-out, branch-both-sides, adversarial structures), autonomy postures, and liveness policies, then set up the playbook + run-log state files and drive the run. Use when the user says "orchestrate this plan", "run this as a relay", "spawn phase agents", "split this across agents/sessions", "herd this plan", or hands over a substantial implementation plan / long runbook that won't fit one context window. Companion to the `herder` skill, which owns the spawn/send/wait/cull mechanics.
- using-napkins: Per-branch working memory in `napkins/` (gitignored). Owns capture, harvest, prune. Use on intents like "capture this", "park this", "save for later", "remember this for the harvest" — recording todos / decisions / questions / lessons from recent conversation without promoting them yet. Also use on end-of-branch intents — "raising a PR", "open the PR", "wrap up the branch", "before merge", "harvest the napkin", "prune the napkin" — to route entries to their permanent homes and delete `napkins/`. Other skills (handoff, improve-architecture, debug repros) write hand-named files into the same directory.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Architecture review brief — rust-raw single-sequencer ("spine")

## Your job
Produce a **prioritized architecture recommendation** for squeezing more performance
out of this matching-engine sequencer, with NO compromise on correctness, performance,
or latency. Two priorities dominate everything else:

1. **Durability + replay**: the response log (`.obox`) must become a committed journal
   with a real durability story (fsync / group commit / crash safety) AND a corresponding
   **replay/recovery** path that reconstructs engine state from the journal on restart.
2. **Zero-copy across the board**: network stack → rings → engine → disk. Eliminate the
   per-message copies and heap allocations on the hot path.

Do NOT write implementation code. Produce a design/recommendation document: concrete
mechanisms, the tradeoffs, ordering of work, and — critically — how each change keeps the
golden fixtures and benchmarks honest (what could silently break correctness or the
latency gate, and how you'd guard it).

## Where things are
Repo root for this experiment: `experiments/single-sequencer/`
Engine under review: `experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/` (crate name `spine`).
Reference worth reading: `experiments/single-sequencer/docs/tigerbeetle-harvest.md`,
`docs/system-design.md`, `contract/boundary-adapter.md`, and the golden fixtures under
`contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`.

## Current architecture (verified)
- 3-thread pipeline in `src/net.rs` + `src/sequencer.rs`:
  `reader_loop` (TCP → ingress ring) → `run_core` (sequencer fold → outbox + ack ring)
  → `writer_loop` (ack ring → TCP).
- `src/ring.rs`: SPSC ring, fixed `SLOT_CAP = 4096`-byte slots. `try_push` **copies**
  the payload into the slot; `try_pop` **allocates a fresh `Vec` and copies out**
  (`slot.bytes[..len].to_vec()`). So every message is copied+allocated on BOTH the
  ingress and ack hops.
- `src/net.rs`: `read_frame_eof` allocates `vec![0u8; len]` per frame and then
  `payload[1..].to_vec()` again. `writer_loop` builds a fresh `Vec` per frame and does
  `write_all` + `flush` per message (syscall per message; no vectored I/O, no batching,
  no TCP_NODELAY set explicitly).
- `src/outbox.rs`: `BufWriter<File>`; each `append_*` allocates a fresh `Vec`, encodes a
  log frame, writes. **`flush()` is called only once, after the run_core loop exits**
  (`src/sequencer.rs:326`). **There is no `fsync` anywhere** → today the journal is not
  durable, and there is **no replay/recovery path** that reads `.obox` back to rebuild state.
- `src/sequencer.rs` (~6800 lines): heavily optimized compute core — dedup arenas, slab
  storage, in-place fold with rollback snapshots, FxHasher maps. This part is already fast;
  treat it as mostly fixed but flag anything that blocks zero-copy or durability.

## Performance context (from prior runs)
- Compute spine currently ~p99.9 3.8µs flat; target is ~1µs/order.
- Latency gate is measured with amortized batch timing + rdtscp; p50/p99/p99.9 are the
  gate. Tail beyond that on a shared box is descheduling noise, not engine.

## Honesty harness (must stay green)
- Golden fixtures: byte-frozen `.obox` outputs under `contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`
  — any change to emitted bytes must be intentional and re-frozen, never silently diverge.
- Benchmarks: `benches/fast_path.rs` (criterion fast-path + sustained tail), rdtscp clock.
- `cargo test --lib`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`.

## Conventions (AGENTS.md — follow in your writeup)
- NO opaque identifiers (no D1/F2/Option-A/rung-4, no order-encoded tags). Name each
  decision/option by a short descriptive phrase or a Named Capitalised Term that carries
  its meaning.
- Write current-state prose, not a changelog.

## What to deliver
A ranked set of recommendations. For each: what it changes, the mechanism, expected
latency/throughput/durability effect, the correctness risk and how the golden+bench
harness catches it, and rough effort. Call out anything where durability and zero-copy
pull against each other (e.g. fsync latency vs the 1µs budget), and how you'd resolve it
(group commit, async-commit with bounded loss window, mmap+msync, io_uring, etc.).
Be specific to THIS codebase, not generic advice.

content.content:
1	I could not re-open the files because command execution is failing before process start in this sandbox, so this review is based on the verified code facts in the brief and the named repo surfaces. The recommendations are still specific to `spine`’s current pipeline and contracts.
2	
3	**Highest Priority: Committed Journal With Replay**
4	
5	The `.obox` file should stop being “outbox bytes eventually flushed” and become the **Committed Response Journal**: the single durable source of truth for responses and restart recovery.
6	
7	Today `src/outbox.rs` writes encoded response frames through `BufWriter<File>`, and `src/sequencer.rs` flushes only after `run_core` exits. That means an acknowledged response can be lost on process crash or power loss. There is also no reader that rebuilds sequencer state from `.obox`, so even a perfectly flushed file is not yet a recovery mechanism.
8	
9	The concrete change is to split response emission into:
10	
11	- a journal encoder that produces the existing byte-frozen `.obox` frame format;
12	- a commit coordinator that batches frames and calls `fdatasync` or `fsync`;
13	- a replay reader that validates frame boundaries and CRC before feeding recovered responses back into engine state reconstruction.
14	
15	The golden fixtures should continue to assert byte identity for the encoded frame stream. Durability metadata must not silently perturb the frozen `.obox` bytes unless the project intentionally freezes a new journal format. If commit markers are needed, prefer an append-compatible trailer or a separate sidecar commit index rather than changing every response frame.
16	
17	The core rule should be: **the writer thread must not acknowledge a response as durable until the journal commit boundary containing that response has reached stable storage**. If the current TCP writer sends acknowledgements directly from the ack ring, the durable path needs a new ordering point:
18	
19	```text
20	sequencer fold -> journal append buffer -> durable commit -> ack publish -> TCP write
21	```
22	
23	For low-latency modes, this creates the main tension in the design: real `fsync` latency is not compatible with a one microsecond per-order budget. The correct resolution is not pretending `fsync` is cheap. It is to make durability mode explicit:
24	
25	- **Strict Durable Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements wait for group commit. Correct after crash, higher tail latency.
26	- **Bounded Loss Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements may be sent before `fsync`, with a documented maximum loss window. This is lower latency but not a committed-journal acknowledgement.
27	- **Replicated Commit Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements wait for quorum replication instead of local disk alone, if the broader system later supports this.
28	
29	For this engine, the first milestone should implement Strict Durable Acknowledgement even if it misses the microsecond gate. That gives the system a correct baseline. Only after that should the lower-latency commit policy be benchmarked and named honestly.
30	
31	Replay should be a first-class binary path, not a test-only parser. On startup, `spine` should open the Committed Response Journal, scan complete frames, verify CRCs using `wire/mod.rs` framing rules, stop at the first torn frame, and reconstruct enough engine state to resume deterministically. The recovery reader must distinguish:
32	
33	- clean end of file;
34	- torn final frame after crash;
35	- corrupted committed frame;
36	- schema or version mismatch;
37	- duplicate or replayed client operation.
38	
39	Correctness risk is high: replay can accidentally accept partial frames, rebuild less state than the live fold maintains, or diverge from dedup arenas and slab state. The guard is a recovery harness that runs the golden inputs through live execution, persists `.obox`, restarts from it, and then proves the next accepted order produces the same response bytes as an uninterrupted run. The existing frozen `.obox` fixtures catch byte drift; they do not by themselves prove recovery equivalence.
40	
41	Effort: large. This touches `outbox.rs`, `sequencer.rs` response plumbing, startup lifecycle, tests, and benchmark modes.
42	
43	**Next Priority: Zero-Copy Ring Ownership**
44	
45	`src/ring.rs` is currently the most obvious hot-path tax. `try_push` copies into a fixed slot and `try_pop` allocates a fresh `Vec`. That means ingress and ack both pay copy plus heap allocation per message.
46	
47	Replace the current byte-copy SPSC ring with a **Buffer Ownership Ring**. The ring should move ownership of preallocated buffers or descriptors, not payload bytes. A practical shape for this codebase is:
48	
49	```text
50	network reader owns free buffers
51	reader fills buffer from TCP
52	reader publishes buffer descriptor to ingress ring
53	sequencer borrows payload from descriptor
54	sequencer publishes response descriptor to journal/ack path
55	writer returns buffers to free pool after send
56	```
57	
58	The slots can still be fixed-capacity at first, preserving the current `SLOT_CAP = 4096` constraint, but the payload should stay in the same backing allocation across network, engine, and writer. The descriptor carries length, message kind, sequence metadata, and ownership state.
59	
60	The engine should consume `&[u8]` or a small `FrameRef` rather than `Vec<u8>`. Any place in `sequencer.rs` that requires ownership should be audited: ownership is acceptable only for data that must outlive the frame. Parsed fields should be copied into engine-owned compact state only when they are semantically stored, not merely because the pipeline API forces a `Vec`.
61	
62	Correctness risk is medium-high: descriptor reuse can create use-after-return bugs, stale lengths, and accidental mutation of bytes that are still needed for journaling or CRC. Rust can help if the API makes buffer return explicit and prevents aliasing. Avoid exposing raw mutable slices after publish. Tests should include poisoned-buffer reuse checks: after a frame is consumed, overwrite the buffer before the next stage would incorrectly read it.
63	
64	Benchmarks must separate allocation removal from compute speed. Add counters for hot-path allocations, or run with an allocator profiler in the benchmark harness. The latency gate should fail if a regression reintroduces per-message heap allocation.
65	
66	Effort: medium-large. The change is conceptually local to `ring.rs`, `net.rs`, and response plumbing, but it will expose assumptions in `sequencer.rs`.
67	
68	**Next Priority: Network Framing Without Per-Message Allocation**
69	
70	`src/net.rs` currently allocates `vec![0u8; len]`, then copies `payload[1..].to_vec()`. The writer builds another fresh `Vec`, then calls `write_all` and `flush` per message.
71	
72	The reader should parse directly into buffers supplied by the Buffer Ownership Ring. Read the frame header into a small stack buffer, reserve a ring buffer slot, then read the payload into the slot’s writable region. The command discriminator can be represented as an offset into the same buffer instead of slicing into a new `Vec`.
73	
74	The writer should use vectored I/O or a reusable output buffer. If the on-wire frame is length prefix plus payload plus CRC, `write_vectored` can send header and payload without assembling a new contiguous `Vec`. If the OS path benefits from contiguous writes, use a per-writer scratch buffer reused across messages, not per-message allocation.
75	
76	The per-message `flush` should go away unless the stream is actually wrapped in a buffered writer. For `TcpStream`, `write_all` already enters the kernel; flushing a wrapper per frame defeats batching. Set `TCP_NODELAY` explicitly so latency behavior is intentional, then benchmark both immediate small writes and bounded micro-batches.
77	
78	Correctness risk is mostly framing drift: off-by-one payload slices, CRC computed over the wrong bytes, or changed flush behavior hiding partial writes. The golden `.obox` fixtures catch response bytes, but not necessarily TCP framing. Add wire-level fixture tests for `wire/mod.rs` that freeze representative input and output frames, including maximum `SLOT_CAP`, empty payload rejection, bad CRC, and truncated frame cases.
79	
80	Effort: medium.
81	
82	**Next Priority: Journal Encoding Without Fresh Allocation**
83	
84	`src/outbox.rs` currently allocates a fresh `Vec` for every appended response. That defeats zero-copy at the disk boundary and creates allocator noise in the benchmark.
85	
86	The journal path should encode into either:
87	
88	- the same response buffer that will later be acknowledged;
89	- a preallocated per-core journal staging buffer;
90	- or a fixed ring of journal segments used by the commit coordinator.
91	
92	The best fit is probably a **Journal Segment Ring**: larger preallocated byte slabs, each containing many encoded `.obox` frames. `ResponseSink` appends frames into the current segment, and the commit coordinator writes complete segment ranges to disk using `write_all`, `write_vectored`, or eventually `io_uring`.
93	
94	This keeps the golden fixture contract intact because the encoded bytes remain the same; only the storage and flushing mechanics change. The fixture harness should compare the concatenated committed journal bytes to the frozen `.obox` files. It should not care how many internal segments were used.
95	
96	Correctness risk is torn-frame handling. Every journal frame needs enough length and CRC structure for replay to identify the last complete valid frame. If the existing `wire/mod.rs` framing already provides that, reuse it exactly. Do not invent a second checksum scheme for `.obox` unless the current CRC does not cover the bytes replay needs to trust.
97	
98	Effort: medium.
99	
100	**Next Priority: ResponseSink As The Durability Boundary**
101	
102	`ResponseSink` plumbing in `sequencer.rs` should become the narrow contract between the compute core and the outside world. The compute core should not know whether responses go to a `BufWriter`, a journal segment, a benchmark sink, or a replay verifier.
103	
104	The contract should express three facts explicitly:
105	
106	- the response bytes are encoded deterministically;
107	- the response has been staged for commit;
108	- the response is eligible for acknowledgement only after the selected commit policy permits it.
109	
110	That means the existing sink likely needs to return a response handle or commit position, not just accept bytes. The ack ring should carry committed response descriptors, not independently encoded ack payloads. This avoids a split-brain path where disk bytes and TCP bytes are separately allocated and separately encoded.
111	
112	Correctness risk is accidental reordering. If batching lets later responses commit before earlier responses, replay and client-visible ordering can diverge. The commit coordinator should publish acknowledgements in journal order, even if writes complete in larger batches.
113	
114	The golden fixtures catch byte order. Add a benchmark-mode assertion that the number of committed responses equals the number of published acknowledgements and that commit positions are monotonic.
115	
116	Effort: medium-large.
117	
118	**Next Priority: Replay-Driven Golden Fixtures**
119	
120	The current frozen `.obox` fixtures are necessary but insufficient once `.obox` becomes the Committed Response Journal. They prove emitted bytes, not crash recovery.
121	
122	Add a recovery fixture mode with the same frozen files:
123	
124	```text
125	read frozen .obox
126	validate every frame and CRC
127	rebuild sequencer state
128	run a deterministic suffix of new commands
129	compare emitted suffix responses with uninterrupted execution
130	```
131	
132	Also add crash-cut tests over generated journals. For every byte offset near frame boundaries, replay should either recover through the last complete committed frame or reject corruption deterministically. A torn final frame should be tolerated only if it is beyond the last committed boundary.
133	
134	This is how the harness stays honest: any journal format change must update both byte fixtures and replay expectations. Any optimization that skips a stored field will fail when recovered state produces a different next response.
135	
136	Effort: medium.
137	
138	**Next Priority: Benchmark Modes That Do Not Lie**
139	
140	The existing `benches/fast_path.rs` and rdtscp timing are appropriate for the compute spine, but durable I/O and zero-copy need separate gates.
141	
142	Keep the pure compute benchmark, because it protects the optimized `sequencer.rs` fold. Add distinct benchmark profiles:
143	
144	- **Hot Path Without Durable Commit**: network/ring/engine/journal staging with no `fsync`, measuring allocation removal and copy reduction.
145	- **Strict Durable Commit**: group commit enabled, measuring realistic acknowledgement latency.
146	- **Replay Throughput**: journal scan and state rebuild speed.
147	- **Writer Micro-Batch**: ack ring to TCP using vectored writes or bounded batches.
148	
149	Do not mix these into one number. The one microsecond target is plausible only for compute plus memory-resident staging, not for stable media commit. The architecture should report both “sequenced” latency and “durably acknowledged” latency.
150	
151	Correctness risk is benchmark dishonesty: accidentally timing only enqueue while the real work happens later. Every benchmark should consume the committed or written result in a way the optimizer cannot remove, and durable benchmarks should verify the file length and final sync result.
152	
153	Effort: small-medium.
154	
155	**Lower Priority: io_uring And mmap After The Contract Is Correct**
156	
157	`io_uring`, `mmap`, `msync`, direct I/O, and registered buffers are tempting, but they should come after the journal/replay contract and buffer ownership model are correct.
158	
159	For this codebase, `io_uring` is attractive once the Journal Segment Ring exists, because registered buffers line up with zero-copy segment ownership and async commit. It can reduce syscall overhead for batched writes and fsync operations. But it also complicates completion ordering, buffer lifetime, and error handling.
160	
161	`mmap` is less obviously right for the first durable journal. It can remove explicit write copies, but `msync` semantics and page-cache writeback behavior are easy to misunderstand. It also makes torn-write reasoning less direct than append-only `write` plus `fdatasync`.
162	
163	The recommended path is:
164	
165	- first use ordinary append-only file writes plus `fdatasync`;
166	- then add group commit;
167	- then consider `io_uring` with registered journal segments;
168	- only consider `mmap` if benchmarks prove syscall overhead, not storage latency, is the bottleneck.
169	
170	Effort: large, and not first.
171	
172	**Recommended Order**
173	
174	Start with the Committed Response Journal and replay reader, even though it will expose latency cost. Then replace byte-copy rings with owned buffer descriptors. Then remove network and outbox per-message allocation. Then tighten `ResponseSink` so journal commit and ack publication cannot diverge. Finally, add advanced async disk I/O only after the simpler durable path is correct and measured.
175

That gives `spine` a path to lower latency without losing the ability to prove what happened after a crash.

I could not re-open the files because command execution is failing before process start in this sandbox, so this review is based on the verified code facts in the brief and the named repo surfaces. The recommendations are still specific to `spine`’s current pipeline and contracts.

**Highest Priority: Committed Journal With Replay**

The `.obox` file should stop being “outbox bytes eventually flushed” and become the **Committed Response Journal**: the single durable source of truth for responses and restart recovery.

Today `src/outbox.rs` writes encoded response frames through `BufWriter<File>`, and `src/sequencer.rs` flushes only after `run_core` exits. That means an acknowledged response can be lost on process crash or power loss. There is also no reader that rebuilds sequencer state from `.obox`, so even a perfectly flushed file is not yet a recovery mechanism.

The concrete change is to split response emission into:

- a journal encoder that produces the existing byte-frozen `.obox` frame format;
- a commit coordinator that batches frames and calls `fdatasync` or `fsync`;
- a replay reader that validates frame boundaries and CRC before feeding recovered responses back into engine state reconstruction.

The golden fixtures should continue to assert byte identity for the encoded frame stream. Durability metadata must not silently perturb the frozen `.obox` bytes unless the project intentionally freezes a new journal format. If commit markers are needed, prefer an append-compatible trailer or a separate sidecar commit index rather than changing every response frame.

The core rule should be: **the writer thread must not acknowledge a response as durable until the journal commit boundary containing that response has reached stable storage**. If the current TCP writer sends acknowledgements directly from the ack ring, the durable path needs a new ordering point:

```text
sequencer fold -> journal append buffer -> durable commit -> ack publish -> TCP write
```

For low-latency modes, this creates the main tension in the design: real `fsync` latency is not compatible with a one microsecond per-order budget. The correct resolution is not pretending `fsync` is cheap. It is to make durability mode explicit:

- **Strict Durable Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements wait for group commit. Correct after crash, higher tail latency.
- **Bounded Loss Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements may be sent before `fsync`, with a documented maximum loss window. This is lower latency but not a committed-journal acknowledgement.
- **Replicated Commit Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements wait for quorum replication instead of local disk alone, if the broader system later supports this.

For this engine, the first milestone should implement Strict Durable Acknowledgement even if it misses the microsecond gate. That gives the system a correct baseline. Only after that should the lower-latency commit policy be benchmarked and named honestly.

Replay should be a first-class binary path, not a test-only parser. On startup, `spine` should open the Committed Response Journal, scan complete frames, verify CRCs using `wire/mod.rs` framing rules, stop at the first torn frame, and reconstruct enough engine state to resume deterministically. The recovery reader must distinguish:

- clean end of file;
- torn final frame after crash;
- corrupted committed frame;
- schema or version mismatch;
- duplicate or replayed client operation.

Correctness risk is high: replay can accidentally accept partial frames, rebuild less state than the live fold maintains, or diverge from dedup arenas and slab state. The guard is a recovery harness that runs the golden inputs through live execution, persists `.obox`, restarts from it, and then proves the next accepted order produces the same response bytes as an uninterrupted run. The existing frozen `.obox` fixtures catch byte drift; they do not by themselves prove recovery equivalence.

Effort: large. This touches `outbox.rs`, `sequencer.rs` response plumbing, startup lifecycle, tests, and benchmark modes.

**Next Priority: Zero-Copy Ring Ownership**

`src/ring.rs` is currently the most obvious hot-path tax. `try_push` copies into a fixed slot and `try_pop` allocates a fresh `Vec`. That means ingress and ack both pay copy plus heap allocation per message.

Replace the current byte-copy SPSC ring with a **Buffer Ownership Ring**. The ring should move ownership of preallocated buffers or descriptors, not payload bytes. A practical shape for this codebase is:

```text
network reader owns free buffers
reader fills buffer from TCP
reader publishes buffer descriptor to ingress ring
sequencer borrows payload from descriptor
sequencer publishes response descriptor to journal/ack path
writer returns buffers to free pool after send
```

The slots can still be fixed-capacity at first, preserving the current `SLOT_CAP = 4096` constraint, but the payload should stay in the same backing allocation across network, engine, and writer. The descriptor carries length, message kind, sequence metadata, and ownership state.

The engine should consume `&[u8]` or a small `FrameRef` rather than `Vec<u8>`. Any place in `sequencer.rs` that requires ownership should be audited: ownership is acceptable only for data that must outlive the frame. Parsed fields should be copied into engine-owned compact state only when they are semantically stored, not merely because the pipeline API forces a `Vec`.

Correctness risk is medium-high: descriptor reuse can create use-after-return bugs, stale lengths, and accidental mutation of bytes that are still needed for journaling or CRC. Rust can help if the API makes buffer return explicit and prevents aliasing. Avoid exposing raw mutable slices after publish. Tests should include poisoned-buffer reuse checks: after a frame is consumed, overwrite the buffer before the next stage would incorrectly read it.

Benchmarks must separate allocation removal from compute speed. Add counters for hot-path allocations, or run with an allocator profiler in the benchmark harness. The latency gate should fail if a regression reintroduces per-message heap allocation.

Effort: medium-large. The change is conceptually local to `ring.rs`, `net.rs`, and response plumbing, but it will expose assumptions in `sequencer.rs`.

**Next Priority: Network Framing Without Per-Message Allocation**

`src/net.rs` currently allocates `vec![0u8; len]`, then copies `payload[1..].to_vec()`. The writer builds another fresh `Vec`, then calls `write_all` and `flush` per message.

The reader should parse directly into buffers supplied by the Buffer Ownership Ring. Read the frame header into a small stack buffer, reserve a ring buffer slot, then read the payload into the slot’s writable region. The command discriminator can be represented as an offset into the same buffer instead of slicing into a new `Vec`.

The writer should use vectored I/O or a reusable output buffer. If the on-wire frame is length prefix plus payload plus CRC, `write_vectored` can send header and payload without assembling a new contiguous `Vec`. If the OS path benefits from contiguous writes, use a per-writer scratch buffer reused across messages, not per-message allocation.

The per-message `flush` should go away unless the stream is actually wrapped in a buffered writer. For `TcpStream`, `write_all` already enters the kernel; flushing a wrapper per frame defeats batching. Set `TCP_NODELAY` explicitly so latency behavior is intentional, then benchmark both immediate small writes and bounded micro-batches.

Correctness risk is mostly framing drift: off-by-one payload slices, CRC computed over the wrong bytes, or changed flush behavior hiding partial writes. The golden `.obox` fixtures catch response bytes, but not necessarily TCP framing. Add wire-level fixture tests for `wire/mod.rs` that freeze representative input and output frames, including maximum `SLOT_CAP`, empty payload rejection, bad CRC, and truncated frame cases.

Effort: medium.

**Next Priority: Journal Encoding Without Fresh Allocation**

`src/outbox.rs` currently allocates a fresh `Vec` for every appended response. That defeats zero-copy at the disk boundary and creates allocator noise in the benchmark.

The journal path should encode into either:

- the same response buffer that will later be acknowledged;
- a preallocated per-core journal staging buffer;
- or a fixed ring of journal segments used by the commit coordinator.

The best fit is probably a **Journal Segment Ring**: larger preallocated byte slabs, each containing many encoded `.obox` frames. `ResponseSink` appends frames into the current segment, and the commit coordinator writes complete segment ranges to disk using `write_all`, `write_vectored`, or eventually `io_uring`.

This keeps the golden fixture contract intact because the encoded bytes remain the same; only the storage and flushing mechanics change. The fixture harness should compare the concatenated committed journal bytes to the frozen `.obox` files. It should not care how many internal segments were used.

Correctness risk is torn-frame handling. Every journal frame needs enough length and CRC structure for replay to identify the last complete valid frame. If the existing `wire/mod.rs` framing already provides that, reuse it exactly. Do not invent a second checksum scheme for `.obox` unless the current CRC does not cover the bytes replay needs to trust.

Effort: medium.

**Next Priority: ResponseSink As The Durability Boundary**

`ResponseSink` plumbing in `sequencer.rs` should become the narrow contract between the compute core and the outside world. The compute core should not know whether responses go to a `BufWriter`, a journal segment, a benchmark sink, or a replay verifier.

The contract should express three facts explicitly:

- the response bytes are encoded deterministically;
- the response has been staged for commit;
- the response is eligible for acknowledgement only after the selected commit policy permits it.

That means the existing sink likely needs to return a response handle or commit position, not just accept bytes. The ack ring should carry committed response descriptors, not independently encoded ack payloads. This avoids a split-brain path where disk bytes and TCP bytes are separately allocated and separately encoded.

Correctness risk is accidental reordering. If batching lets later responses commit before earlier responses, replay and client-visible ordering can diverge. The commit coordinator should publish acknowledgements in journal order, even if writes complete in larger batches.

The golden fixtures catch byte order. Add a benchmark-mode assertion that the number of committed responses equals the number of published acknowledgements and that commit positions are monotonic.

Effort: medium-large.

**Next Priority: Replay-Driven Golden Fixtures**

The current frozen `.obox` fixtures are necessary but insufficient once `.obox` becomes the Committed Response Journal. They prove emitted bytes, not crash recovery.

Add a recovery fixture mode with the same frozen files:

```text
read frozen .obox
validate every frame and CRC
rebuild sequencer state
run a deterministic suffix of new commands
compare emitted suffix responses with uninterrupted execution
```

Also add crash-cut tests over generated journals. For every byte offset near frame boundaries, replay should either recover through the last complete committed frame or reject corruption deterministically. A torn final frame should be tolerated only if it is beyond the last committed boundary.

This is how the harness stays honest: any journal format change must update both byte fixtures and replay expectations. Any optimization that skips a stored field will fail when recovered state produces a different next response.

Effort: medium.

**Next Priority: Benchmark Modes That Do Not Lie**

The existing `benches/fast_path.rs` and rdtscp timing are appropriate for the compute spine, but durable I/O and zero-copy need separate gates.

Keep the pure compute benchmark, because it protects the optimized `sequencer.rs` fold. Add distinct benchmark profiles:

- **Hot Path Without Durable Commit**: network/ring/engine/journal staging with no `fsync`, measuring allocation removal and copy reduction.
- **Strict Durable Commit**: group commit enabled, measuring realistic acknowledgement latency.
- **Replay Throughput**: journal scan and state rebuild speed.
- **Writer Micro-Batch**: ack ring to TCP using vectored writes or bounded batches.

Do not mix these into one number. The one microsecond target is plausible only for compute plus memory-resident staging, not for stable media commit. The architecture should report both “sequenced” latency and “durably acknowledged” latency.

Correctness risk is benchmark dishonesty: accidentally timing only enqueue while the real work happens later. Every benchmark should consume the committed or written result in a way the optimizer cannot remove, and durable benchmarks should verify the file length and final sync result.

Effort: small-medium.

**Lower Priority: io_uring And mmap After The Contract Is Correct**

`io_uring`, `mmap`, `msync`, direct I/O, and registered buffers are tempting, but they should come after the journal/replay contract and buffer ownership model are correct.

For this codebase, `io_uring` is attractive once the Journal Segment Ring exists, because registered buffers line up with zero-copy segment ownership and async commit. It can reduce syscall overhead for batched writes and fsync operations. But it also complicates completion ordering, buffer lifetime, and error handling.

`mmap` is less obviously right for the first durable journal. It can remove explicit write copies, but `msync` semantics and page-cache writeback behavior are easy to misunderstand. It also makes torn-write reasoning less direct than append-only `write` plus `fdatasync`.

The recommended path is:

- first use ordinary append-only file writes plus `fdatasync`;
- then add group commit;
- then consider `io_uring` with registered journal segments;
- only consider `mmap` if benchmarks prove syscall overhead, not storage latency, is the bottleneck.

Effort: large, and not first.

**Recommended Order**

Start with the Committed Response Journal and replay reader, even though it will expose latency cost. Then replace byte-copy rings with owned buffer descriptors. Then remove network and outbox per-message allocation. Then tighten `ResponseSink` so journal commit and ack publication cannot diverge. Finally, add advanced async disk I/O only after the simpler durable path is correct and measured.

The key architectural point is that durability and zero-copy should share one design: responses move through owned buffers into journal segments, journal commit publishes ack eligibility, and replay reads the exact same committed byte stream that golden fixtures freeze. That gives `spine` a path to lower latency without losing the ability to prove what happened after a crash.

# Architecture recommendation — rust-raw single-sequencer (`spine`)

## Framing: two findings that reshape the brief

Before the ranked list, two facts from the code change what "durability + replay" and "zero-copy" actually mean here:

**The `.obox` is derived output, not a replay source.** The brief asks that the response log "become a committed journal AND a replay/recovery path that reconstructs engine state from the journal." But `.obox` holds *post-fold outcome frames* (fills, status, balances), and the contract itself records that two pieces of authoritative state — the per-market open-interest counter and the dedup-key set — have *no Block-3 Response witness* (`contract/boundary-adapter.md:122`). You therefore cannot rebuild `Sequencer` state by replaying `.obox`. The deterministic source of truth is the **input command stream** (`docs/system-design.md:117-123, 327`), and rust-raw does not durably persist it anywhere: input frames land in the ingress ring (`src/net.rs:220`) and are dropped after the fold. So priority (1) is not "add fsync to `outbox.rs`" — it is "introduce a durable input/sequence log that today does not exist, and make *that* the replay source." The `.obox` is a second, derived durable artifact that must be hardened separately for exactly-once.

**The latency gate is blind to the transport.** `benches/fast_path.rs` drives `accept_with_sink` directly with an in-memory `BenchSink` (`benches/fast_path.rs:283-306, 362`). It never touches the ring, the TCP framing, the ack path, or disk. So the per-command allocations in `ring.rs`, `net.rs`, and the ack path (below) **do not appear in the ~3.8µs/1µs number at all**, and fsync is entirely outside it. This cuts both ways: zero-copy work on the transport will not move the headline gate (so it cannot be validated by it), and adding fsync will not regress the gate either (so the gate cannot protect the durability budget). Every recommendation below names the *additional* honesty instrument needed because the current gate cannot see the change.

---

## Ranked recommendations

### 1. Introduce the durable sequence log as the replay source of truth
**Changes:** A new append-only input journal that captures each sequenced command's raw bytes, plus a startup replay path. This is the precondition for all of priority (1); nothing else delivers recovery.

**Mechanism:** "One binary format end to end" (`docs/system-design.md:60-67`) means the ingress payload *is* the log record — no re-encode. In `run_core`, the raw `item.bytes` popped from the ingress ring (`src/sequencer.rs:307`) is exactly what to frame (`seq`, `crc32c`, parent-chain — see item 3) and append. Recovery on startup: validate the header (`wire::validate_obox_header`), walk frames with `validate_log_frame` (`src/wire/mod.rs:434`), and feed each record body back through `Sequencer::accept` — which rebuilds the matcher, accounts, OI counter, *and* the dedup map (`src/sequencer.rs:193-237`) for free, because dedup already lives in journaled state.

**Effect:** Unlocks crash recovery and replay-equivalence. Durability is bounded by the journaler's fsync policy (item 2), not the fold. No throughput cost on the fold thread if journaling is off-thread (item 2).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Replaying the input log must reproduce byte-identical `.obox`. Guard with a replay-equivalence test that runs each golden input stream, then *replays the captured input log into a fresh process* and asserts the regenerated `.obox` diffs clean against the frozen fixture (`contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`). This is the TigerBeetle StateChecker re-aimed at cross-time equivalence (`docs/tigerbeetle-harvest.md:157`).

**Effort:** Medium-Large.

### 2. Re-split the journaler onto its own cursor with group-commit fsync; release acks on the durable cursor
**Changes:** Restore the three-consumer ring topology the spec prescribes (`docs/system-design.md:126-153`) that rust-raw collapsed. Today the core thread folds *and* writes the outbox inline via the `Outbox` sink (`src/sequencer.rs:308`), and the ack is pushed to the ack ring *before* anything is durable (`src/sequencer.rs:313`). That collapse is fine for a flush-only stub but breaks the moment fsync enters: an inline `fdatasync` would land directly on the fold's 1µs budget.

**Mechanism:** A dedicated journaler consumer reads the sequenced stream, appends to the input log (item 1), and issues **one `fdatasync` per group-commit batch** — flush on fill or ~100µs, whichever first (`docs/system-design.md:155-168`, `tigerbeetle-harvest.md:200`). The journaler advances a `durable_cursor`; the SEQUENCED ack is released by the journaler *after* the batch's fsync, not by the fold (`docs/system-design.md:143-148`). The matcher runs ahead in memory; no `.obox` frame escapes past `min(matching_cursor, durable_cursor)`.

**Effect:** This is the resolution of the fsync-vs-1µs tension (expanded below). Fold latency stays compute-bound (~1µs target unaffected); ack latency absorbs the amortized fsync tail; throughput scales with batch size at ~2M cmd/s.

**Correctness risk + harness:** The harness still expects exactly one SEQUENCED per input (`boundary-adapter.md` obligation 3) — now delivered later and in batches, but unchanged in content; goldens stay byte-identical (I/O is below the determinism boundary, `tigerbeetle-harvest.md:200`). New instrument: a crash-injection test biased to the in-flight fsync window (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:159`) asserting no acked command is ever lost and no un-acked command is ever durable.

**Effort:** Large (it is a structural re-split, plus a second cursor and backpressure).

### 3. Per-record hash-chain, trailing-seal framing, and the three-case recovery policy
**Changes:** Make both logs self-verifying and give recovery a precise torn-tail vs interior-corruption decision. The current frame carries a self-CRC over `seq+record` and a self-locating `seq` (`src/wire/mod.rs:423-431`) — good — but **no parent chain**, so a spliced or reordered region is undetectable, and there is no recovery policy at all.

**Mechanism:** Add `parent = checksum(previous_frame)` (genesis 0) to the log frame (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:136`); the chain head at seq N becomes a single compact equivalence witness. Recovery implements three cases (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:139`): clean; torn tail (CRC fails *at* the durable cursor → truncate to last chain-consistent record — nothing past the ack boundary was promised); interior corruption (CRC fails *inside* the fsync'd region → halt and declare loss, never silently truncate). Add the cheap single-node gaps: `fsync(fd)` on startup before any read, and `fsync(dir_fd)` after creating each segment (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:201`).

**Effect:** Turns "the log is truth" into a checkable claim; negligible hot-path cost (one extra checksum per record, already CRC'ing).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Adding the parent field **changes every emitted byte** — this is an intentional reframe of the frozen format, so re-freeze all `frozen-obox/` fixtures *once* and cross-check the recomputed chain head against an independent implementation so the re-freeze isn't laundering a bug (the `.obox-blind rollback` lesson). Add hand-corrupted golden logs for each recovery case.

**Effort:** Medium (chain) + Medium (recovery walk).

### 4. Zero-copy ingress: read TCP straight into the ring slot
**Changes:** Eliminate the three per-command copies on the ingress hop. Today `read_frame_eof` allocates `vec![0u8; len]` then copies again with `payload[1..].to_vec()` (`src/net.rs:308,314`), and `try_push` copies the payload a third time into the slot (`src/ring.rs:101`), and `try_pop` allocates a fresh `Vec` via `slot.bytes[..len].to_vec()` (`src/ring.rs:137`).

**Mechanism:** Two new ring APIs. A fill-in-place producer (`reserve` a slot, hand `&mut slot.bytes` to the reader so `read_exact` lands the frame body directly in the slot, then publish with the true length) collapses all three copies into one TCP read. A borrowing consumer (a read-guard derefing to `&[u8]` that advances `head` on drop, or a `consume(|bytes, ingress_ns|)` closure) removes the `to_vec`. `accept_with_sink` already takes `&[u8]` (`src/sequencer.rs:173`) and decoding is already zero-copy through the borrowing `Reader` (`src/wire/mod.rs:46-72`), so the fold composes unchanged.

**Effect:** The single largest hot-path allocation win; restores the "zero allocation on the hot path" invariant (`docs/system-design.md:54-57`) on ingress. Caps record size at `SLOT_CAP=4096` (`src/ring.rs:7`) — a documented Capacity-Budget bound (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:177`), already enforced via `RingError::TooLarge` (`src/net.rs:222`).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Must not perturb a single emitted byte — so the frozen `.obox` diff *is* the guard (a corrupted in-place read fails at the first bad `seq`). The gate bench can't see this path, so add (a) a property test feeding random frames through both the in-place ring and the old copy ring asserting identical bytes, and (b) an end-to-end alloc gate using the existing `alloc_counter` (`src/alloc_counter.rs:59`) asserting zero steady-state allocations across net→ring→fold, since the latency gate won't.

**Effort:** Medium.

### 5. Zero-copy and batched egress: ack path and TCP writes
**Changes:** The ack hop mirrors ingress waste — `encode_ack_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` per ack (`src/sequencer.rs:332`), pushed (copy) into the ack ring, popped (`to_vec`), then `writer_loop` builds *another* fresh `Vec` per frame and does `write_all` + `flush` **per message** — a syscall per ack with no `TCP_NODELAY` set anywhere (grep confirms none) (`src/net.rs:257-261`).

**Mechanism:** Encode the ack into a reusable scratch buffer (or directly into the ack-ring slot via the item-4 producer API); on the writer, set `TCP_NODELAY` once, coalesce ready acks and emit with a single `writev`/batched `write`, and flush once per batch rather than per frame. The 50µs sleep-poll (`src/net.rs:271`) stays as the light-load backstop.

**Effect:** Removes ~4 allocations and one syscall per command on egress; batched writes are the throughput lever for the ack stream under load.

**Correctness risk + harness:** Ack *content* and per-input correlation by `Idem` are unchanged (`boundary-adapter.md` obligation 3); batching only changes timing. Same end-to-end alloc gate from item 4 covers it. `TCP_NODELAY` changes wire *timing*, never bytes — goldens unaffected.

**Effort:** Medium.

### 6. Zero-copy outbox encode
**Changes:** Each `append_response_frame`/`append_response_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` per frame before handing it to the `BufWriter` (`src/outbox.rs:26-28,38-42`).

**Mechanism:** Encode the log frame directly into a reusable scratch buffer owned by `Outbox` (or directly into the `BufWriter`'s buffer), reset per frame. `encode_log_frame_record` already takes a closure writing into a caller buffer (`src/wire/mod.rs:415`), so this is a small plumbing change.

**Effect:** Removes N allocations per command (one per emitted response) on the publisher path; small but on the hot path in the real net pipeline.

**Correctness risk + harness:** Pure refactor of byte production — frozen `.obox` diff catches any divergence directly.

**Effort:** Small.

### 7. (Lower priority, optional) O_DIRECT + sector-aligned batch buffers; snapshot with atomic-flip manifest
**Changes:** O_DIRECT page-cache bypass paired with `fdatasync`/FUA per batch (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:199`), removing the failed-fsync-serves-dirty-pages hazard; and a checksummed snapshot with atomic manifest promotion (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:214-217`) to bound restart time (`docs/system-design.md:463-478`).

**Mechanism:** Sector-aligned (4096) pre-allocated batch buffers (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:203`); snapshot via a dedicated replay consumer or COW fork, write-fresh→fsync→verify→flip, prune log only after the new snapshot is durable.

**Effect:** O_DIRECT hardens the durability *failure mode* (not latency); snapshots cut replay tail (~2 min at 5-min interval, `docs/system-design.md:472`). Both are follow-ons, not blockers.

**Correctness risk + harness:** O_DIRECT alignment must pad the partial tail sector — a decision that affects on-disk bytes (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:203`); pin it and re-freeze. Snapshot load must verify checksum and fall back to log-from-zero on mismatch (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:215`) or it resurrects wrong state.

**Effort:** Large each.

---

## Where fsync durability and the ~1µs budget pull against each other — and the resolution

The collision is only apparent, and it exists today *because* rust-raw collapsed the journaler into the fold thread (`src/sequencer.rs:308`). A single NVMe `fdatasync` is ~10µs–1ms; calling it per command, inline, would blow the 1µs fold budget by one to three orders of magnitude, and at 2M cmd/s it is simply impossible.

The resolution is structural, and the spec already names it: **the fold budget and the durability seam live on different cursors and different threads** (`docs/system-design.md:143-168`). The 1µs the gate measures is *compute-path* latency — emit minus ingress in the SUT clock domain (`boundary-adapter.md:154-159`) — and the fold never calls fsync. The fsync tail lives in *ack* latency, the durable-cursor round-trip, which the contract measures as a separate number. Item 2 makes this real: the journaler batches and issues one `fdatasync` per group-commit window (~100µs), so fsync cost is amortized across the whole batch and the per-command durable cost trends toward (fsync tail ÷ batch size) — at 2M cmd/s a 100µs window batches ~200 commands per sync. The matcher runs ahead in memory; acks are released by the journaler post-sync. Group commit is the lever that lets durability and the compute budget coexist *because they were never the same measurement* — they only collide if you keep journaling inline.

Two honesty caveats fall out of this. First, the current gate cannot police the durability budget (it never fsyncs), so item 2 must ship with a *separate* ack-latency/durable-cursor benchmark and the fsync-window crash-injection test (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:159`) — otherwise group-commit regressions are invisible. Second, the interior-corruption policy (item 3) is the one place durability scope and the ack contract genuinely conflict: auto-truncating past corruption would silently drop acked commands (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:139`, open question `:348`). The spec's answer — and the correct one — is halt-and-declare-loss for interior corruption, truncate only the torn tail beyond the durable cursor, because everything inside the fsync'd region was promised to a client.</result>

# Architecture recommendation — rust-raw single-sequencer (`spine`)

## Framing: two findings that reshape the brief

Before the ranked list, two facts from the code change what "durability + replay" and "zero-copy" actually mean here:

**The `.obox` is derived output, not a replay source.** The brief asks that the response log "become a committed journal AND a replay/recovery path that reconstructs engine state from the journal." But `.obox` holds *post-fold outcome frames* (fills, status, balances), and the contract itself records that two pieces of authoritative state — the per-market open-interest counter and the dedup-key set — have *no Block-3 Response witness* (`contract/boundary-adapter.md:122`). You therefore cannot rebuild `Sequencer` state by replaying `.obox`. The deterministic source of truth is the **input command stream** (`docs/system-design.md:117-123, 327`), and rust-raw does not durably persist it anywhere: input frames land in the ingress ring (`src/net.rs:220`) and are dropped after the fold. So priority (1) is not "add fsync to `outbox.rs`" — it is "introduce a durable input/sequence log that today does not exist, and make *that* the replay source." The `.obox` is a second, derived durable artifact that must be hardened separately for exactly-once.

**The latency gate is blind to the transport.** `benches/fast_path.rs` drives `accept_with_sink` directly with an in-memory `BenchSink` (`benches/fast_path.rs:283-306, 362`). It never touches the ring, the TCP framing, the ack path, or disk. So the per-command allocations in `ring.rs`, `net.rs`, and the ack path (below) **do not appear in the ~3.8µs/1µs number at all**, and fsync is entirely outside it. This cuts both ways: zero-copy work on the transport will not move the headline gate (so it cannot be validated by it), and adding fsync will not regress the gate either (so the gate cannot protect the durability budget). Every recommendation below names the *additional* honesty instrument needed because the current gate cannot see the change.

---

## Ranked recommendations

### 1. Introduce the durable sequence log as the replay source of truth
**Changes:** A new append-only input journal that captures each sequenced command's raw bytes, plus a startup replay path. This is the precondition for all of priority (1); nothing else delivers recovery.

**Mechanism:** "One binary format end to end" (`docs/system-design.md:60-67`) means the ingress payload *is* the log record — no re-encode. In `run_core`, the raw `item.bytes` popped from the ingress ring (`src/sequencer.rs:307`) is exactly what to frame (`seq`, `crc32c`, parent-chain — see item 3) and append. Recovery on startup: validate the header (`wire::validate_obox_header`), walk frames with `validate_log_frame` (`src/wire/mod.rs:434`), and feed each record body back through `Sequencer::accept` — which rebuilds the matcher, accounts, OI counter, *and* the dedup map (`src/sequencer.rs:193-237`) for free, because dedup already lives in journaled state.

**Effect:** Unlocks crash recovery and replay-equivalence. Durability is bounded by the journaler's fsync policy (item 2), not the fold. No throughput cost on the fold thread if journaling is off-thread (item 2).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Replaying the input log must reproduce byte-identical `.obox`. Guard with a replay-equivalence test that runs each golden input stream, then *replays the captured input log into a fresh process* and asserts the regenerated `.obox` diffs clean against the frozen fixture (`contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`). This is the TigerBeetle StateChecker re-aimed at cross-time equivalence (`docs/tigerbeetle-harvest.md:157`).

**Effort:** Medium-Large.

### 2. Re-split the journaler onto its own cursor with group-commit fsync; release acks on the durable cursor
**Changes:** Restore the three-consumer ring topology the spec prescribes (`docs/system-design.md:126-153`) that rust-raw collapsed. Today the core thread folds *and* writes the outbox inline via the `Outbox` sink (`src/sequencer.rs:308`), and the ack is pushed to the ack ring *before* anything is durable (`src/sequencer.rs:313`). That collapse is fine for a flush-only stub but breaks the moment fsync enters: an inline `fdatasync` would land directly on the fold's 1µs budget.

**Mechanism:** A dedicated journaler consumer reads the sequenced stream, appends to the input log (item 1), and issues **one `fdatasync` per group-commit batch** — flush on fill or ~100µs, whichever first (`docs/system-design.md:155-168`, `tigerbeetle-harvest.md:200`). The journaler advances a `durable_cursor`; the SEQUENCED ack is released by the journaler *after* the batch's fsync, not by the fold (`docs/system-design.md:143-148`). The matcher runs ahead in memory; no `.obox` frame escapes past `min(matching_cursor, durable_cursor)`.

**Effect:** This is the resolution of the fsync-vs-1µs tension (expanded below). Fold latency stays compute-bound (~1µs target unaffected); ack latency absorbs the amortized fsync tail; throughput scales with batch size at ~2M cmd/s.

**Correctness risk + harness:** The harness still expects exactly one SEQUENCED per input (`boundary-adapter.md` obligation 3) — now delivered later and in batches, but unchanged in content; goldens stay byte-identical (I/O is below the determinism boundary, `tigerbeetle-harvest.md:200`). New instrument: a crash-injection test biased to the in-flight fsync window (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:159`) asserting no acked command is ever lost and no un-acked command is ever durable.

**Effort:** Large (it is a structural re-split, plus a second cursor and backpressure).

### 3. Per-record hash-chain, trailing-seal framing, and the three-case recovery policy
**Changes:** Make both logs self-verifying and give recovery a precise torn-tail vs interior-corruption decision. The current frame carries a self-CRC over `seq+record` and a self-locating `seq` (`src/wire/mod.rs:423-431`) — good — but **no parent chain**, so a spliced or reordered region is undetectable, and there is no recovery policy at all.

**Mechanism:** Add `parent = checksum(previous_frame)` (genesis 0) to the log frame (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:136`); the chain head at seq N becomes a single compact equivalence witness. Recovery implements three cases (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:139`): clean; torn tail (CRC fails *at* the durable cursor → truncate to last chain-consistent record — nothing past the ack boundary was promised); interior corruption (CRC fails *inside* the fsync'd region → halt and declare loss, never silently truncate). Add the cheap single-node gaps: `fsync(fd)` on startup before any read, and `fsync(dir_fd)` after creating each segment (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:201`).

**Effect:** Turns "the log is truth" into a checkable claim; negligible hot-path cost (one extra checksum per record, already CRC'ing).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Adding the parent field **changes every emitted byte** — this is an intentional reframe of the frozen format, so re-freeze all `frozen-obox/` fixtures *once* and cross-check the recomputed chain head against an independent implementation so the re-freeze isn't laundering a bug (the `.obox-blind rollback` lesson). Add hand-corrupted golden logs for each recovery case.

**Effort:** Medium (chain) + Medium (recovery walk).

### 4. Zero-copy ingress: read TCP straight into the ring slot
**Changes:** Eliminate the three per-command copies on the ingress hop. Today `read_frame_eof` allocates `vec![0u8; len]` then copies again with `payload[1..].to_vec()` (`src/net.rs:308,314`), and `try_push` copies the payload a third time into the slot (`src/ring.rs:101`), and `try_pop` allocates a fresh `Vec` via `slot.bytes[..len].to_vec()` (`src/ring.rs:137`).

**Mechanism:** Two new ring APIs. A fill-in-place producer (`reserve` a slot, hand `&mut slot.bytes` to the reader so `read_exact` lands the frame body directly in the slot, then publish with the true length) collapses all three copies into one TCP read. A borrowing consumer (a read-guard derefing to `&[u8]` that advances `head` on drop, or a `consume(|bytes, ingress_ns|)` closure) removes the `to_vec`. `accept_with_sink` already takes `&[u8]` (`src/sequencer.rs:173`) and decoding is already zero-copy through the borrowing `Reader` (`src/wire/mod.rs:46-72`), so the fold composes unchanged.

**Effect:** The single largest hot-path allocation win; restores the "zero allocation on the hot path" invariant (`docs/system-design.md:54-57`) on ingress. Caps record size at `SLOT_CAP=4096` (`src/ring.rs:7`) — a documented Capacity-Budget bound (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:177`), already enforced via `RingError::TooLarge` (`src/net.rs:222`).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Must not perturb a single emitted byte — so the frozen `.obox` diff *is* the guard (a corrupted in-place read fails at the first bad `seq`). The gate bench can't see this path, so add (a) a property test feeding random frames through both the in-place ring and the old copy ring asserting identical bytes, and (b) an end-to-end alloc gate using the existing `alloc_counter` (`src/alloc_counter.rs:59`) asserting zero steady-state allocations across net→ring→fold, since the latency gate won't.

**Effort:** Medium.

### 5. Zero-copy and batched egress: ack path and TCP writes
**Changes:** The ack hop mirrors ingress waste — `encode_ack_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` per ack (`src/sequencer.rs:332`), pushed (copy) into the ack ring, popped (`to_vec`), then `writer_loop` builds *another* fresh `Vec` per frame and does `write_all` + `flush` **per message** — a syscall per ack with no `TCP_NODELAY` set anywhere (grep confirms none) (`src/net.rs:257-261`).

**Mechanism:** Encode the ack into a reusable scratch buffer (or directly into the ack-ring slot via the item-4 producer API); on the writer, set `TCP_NODELAY` once, coalesce ready acks and emit with a single `writev`/batched `write`, and flush once per batch rather than per frame. The 50µs sleep-poll (`src/net.rs:271`) stays as the light-load backstop.

**Effect:** Removes ~4 allocations and one syscall per command on egress; batched writes are the throughput lever for the ack stream under load.

**Correctness risk + harness:** Ack *content* and per-input correlation by `Idem` are unchanged (`boundary-adapter.md` obligation 3); batching only changes timing. Same end-to-end alloc gate from item 4 covers it. `TCP_NODELAY` changes wire *timing*, never bytes — goldens unaffected.

**Effort:** Medium.

### 6. Zero-copy outbox encode
**Changes:** Each `append_response_frame`/`append_response_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` per frame before handing it to the `BufWriter` (`src/outbox.rs:26-28,38-42`).

**Mechanism:** Encode the log frame directly into a reusable scratch buffer owned by `Outbox` (or directly into the `BufWriter`'s buffer), reset per frame. `encode_log_frame_record` already takes a closure writing into a caller buffer (`src/wire/mod.rs:415`), so this is a small plumbing change.

**Effect:** Removes N allocations per command (one per emitted response) on the publisher path; small but on the hot path in the real net pipeline.

**Correctness risk + harness:** Pure refactor of byte production — frozen `.obox` diff catches any divergence directly.

**Effort:** Small.

### 7. (Lower priority, optional) O_DIRECT + sector-aligned batch buffers; snapshot with atomic-flip manifest
**Changes:** O_DIRECT page-cache bypass paired with `fdatasync`/FUA per batch (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:199`), removing the failed-fsync-serves-dirty-pages hazard; and a checksummed snapshot with atomic manifest promotion (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:214-217`) to bound restart time (`docs/system-design.md:463-478`).

**Mechanism:** Sector-aligned (4096) pre-allocated batch buffers (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:203`); snapshot via a dedicated replay consumer or COW fork, write-fresh→fsync→verify→flip, prune log only after the new snapshot is durable.

**Effect:** O_DIRECT hardens the durability *failure mode* (not latency); snapshots cut replay tail (~2 min at 5-min interval, `docs/system-design.md:472`). Both are follow-ons, not blockers.

**Correctness risk + harness:** O_DIRECT alignment must pad the partial tail sector — a decision that affects on-disk bytes (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:203`); pin it and re-freeze. Snapshot load must verify checksum and fall back to log-from-zero on mismatch (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:215`) or it resurrects wrong state.

**Effort:** Large each.

---

## Where fsync durability and the ~1µs budget pull against each other — and the resolution

The collision is only apparent, and it exists today *because* rust-raw collapsed the journaler into the fold thread (`src/sequencer.rs:308`). A single NVMe `fdatasync` is ~10µs–1ms; calling it per command, inline, would blow the 1µs fold budget by one to three orders of magnitude, and at 2M cmd/s it is simply impossible.

The resolution is structural, and the spec already names it: **the fold budget and the durability seam live on different cursors and different threads** (`docs/system-design.md:143-168`). The 1µs the gate measures is *compute-path* latency — emit minus ingress in the SUT clock domain (`boundary-adapter.md:154-159`) — and the fold never calls fsync. The fsync tail lives in *ack* latency, the durable-cursor round-trip, which the contract measures as a separate number. Item 2 makes this real: the journaler batches and issues one `fdatasync` per group-commit window (~100µs), so fsync cost is amortized across the whole batch and the per-command durable cost trends toward (fsync tail ÷ batch size) — at 2M cmd/s a 100µs window batches ~200 commands per sync. The matcher runs ahead in memory; acks are released by the journaler post-sync. Group commit is the lever that lets durability and the compute budget coexist *because they were never the same measurement* — they only collide if you keep journaling inline.

Two honesty caveats fall out of this. First, the current gate cannot police the durability budget (it never fsyncs), so item 2 must ship with a *separate* ack-latency/durable-cursor benchmark and the fsync-window crash-injection test (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:159`) — otherwise group-commit regressions are invisible. Second, the interior-corruption policy (item 3) is the one place durability scope and the ack contract genuinely conflict: auto-truncating past corruption would silently drop acked commands (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:139`, open question `:348`). The spec's answer — and the correct one — is halt-and-declare-loss for interior corruption, truncate only the torn tail beyond the durable cursor, because everything inside the fsync'd region was promised to a client.</result>
